g.data-narration: Welcome to Sorting Algorithms! Learn how to efficiently arrange data and choose the right algorithm.
Sorting arranges elements in ascending or descending order based on a comparison criterion. It's fundamental to computer science.
g.text: Sorting is the process of arranging elements in a specific order
(ascending or descending) based on a comparison criterion.

g.data-narration: Sorting enables binary search with O(log n) complexity, organizes data for easier analysis, and is required by many algorithms.
Sorting algorithms are classified as comparison-based or non-comparison. Comparison sorts have a lower bound of Ω(n log n).
In-place algorithms use O(1) extra space like Bubble, Selection, and Quick Sort. Out-of-place like Merge Sort uses O(n).
g.text: O(1) space: Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Quick

g.data-narration: Stable sorting preserves the relative order of equal elements. Merge, Insertion, and Bubble are stable; Quick, Heap, and Selection are not.
Adaptive algorithms perform better on nearly sorted data, like Insertion Sort. Non-adaptive sorts take the same time regardless of input order.
g.text: Faster on nearly sorted data: Insertion, Bubble (optimized)
Same time regardless: Selection, Merge, Heap

g.data-narration: This table shows time and space complexity for all major sorting algorithms. Quick Sort is fastest on average, Merge Sort guarantees O(n log n).
Choose Insertion Sort for small or nearly sorted arrays, Quick Sort for average performance, Merge Sort for stability, and Heap Sort for memory constraints.
An inversion is a pair where i < j but arr[i] > arr[j]. Sorted arrays have 0 inversions, reverse sorted has n(n-1)/2 inversions.
g.text: Number of pairs (i, j) where i < j but arr[i] > arr[j]

g.data-narration: Comparison-based sorting cannot be faster than Ω(n log n) in worst case. This is proven using decision trees with n! possible leaf nodes.
g.text: Decision tree has n! leaves (all possible permutations)
Tree height ≥ log(n!) = Ω(n log n) by Stirling's approximation

g.data-narration: In summary: Know the complexities, stability, and space usage of each algorithm. Quick Sort is fastest in practice, Merge Sort guarantees O(n log n).
g.text: Quick Sort: fastest average | Merge Sort: stable, guaranteed O(n log n) | Insertion Sort: best for small/nearly sorted
Remember: Know complexity, stability, and in-place property for exams!
